coredata.dc:title: Luminescent carbon dots-rooted polysaccharide crosslinked hydrogel adsorbent for sensitive determination and efficient removal of Cu2+
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Jingkun
Wang, Liying
Jiang, Guoyong
Wan, Yuqi
Wang, Jiahua
Li, Yan
Pi, Fuwei
coredata.dc:description: In this study, a novel luminescent carbon dot-rooted polysaccharide hydrogel (CDs@CCP hydrogel) was prepared by crosslinking cellulose, chitosan (CS), and polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) for simultaneous fluorescent sensing and adsorption of Cu2+. The crosslinking of these low-cost, polysaccharide polymers greatly enhance the mechanical strength of the composite hydrogel while making the polysaccharide-based adsorbent easy to reuse. This composite hydrogel exhibited an excellent adsorption capacity (124.7 mg∙g−1) for residual Cu2+ in water, as well as a sensitive and selective fluorescence response towards Cu2+ with a good linear relationship (R2 > 0.97) and a low detection limit (LOD) of 0.02 μM. The adsorption isotherms, adsorption kinetics, and thermodynamics studies were also conducted to investigate the adsorption mechanism. This composite hydrogel offers an efficient tool for simultaneous monitoring and treatment of Cu2+ from wastewater.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Heavy metal ions
Cellulose
Chitosan
Carbon dot
Fluorescent sensing
Cu2+ removal

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication and characterization of CDs@CCP hydrogels
Optical properties of CDs@CCP hydrogels and fluorescent detection of cu 2+
Thermodynamics studies of the adsorption process

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Postgraduate Research & Practice Innovation Program of Jiangsu Province

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors acknowledge the financial support from the Postgraduate Research & Practice Innovation Program of Jiangsu Province (No. KYCX22_2400 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram for fabricating CDs and CDs@CCPs hydrogels.
SEM images of CDs@CCP composite hydrogels.

(a) FT-IR spectra of CDs, CCP, and CDs@CCP hydrogels. (b) Enlarged FT-IR spectra in the range of 1500–1780 cm−1. (c) PXRD patterns of chitosan, microcrystalline cellulose, PVA, and CDs@CCP hydrogels. (d) Excitation and emission spectra of fluorescent CDs@CCP hydrogels. (e) UV–Vis spectrum of neat CD solution (blue line) and photoluminescent excitation spectrum of CDs@CCP hydrogel, respectively. (f) Fluorescence spectra of neat CD solution (red region) and CDs@CCP hydrogel (gray region), respectively. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) Fluorescence quenching efficiency of CDs@CCP composite hydrogel towards different metal ions. (b) The relationship of fluorescence quenching efficiency versus Cu2+ concentration. (c) Pseudo-first-order and (d) pseudo-second-order adsorption kinetics at three different initial Cu2+ concentrations, respectively. (e) Langmuir and (f) Freundlich adsorption isotherm models at three different temperatures, respectively. The concentrations of Cu2+ solutions were set in the range of 25–1000 mg∙L−1.
(a) Possible adsorption mechanism and (b) FT-IR spectra of CDs@CCP hydrogel before and after adsorption of Cu2+.
Comparison of the adsorption performances for Cu2+ with previous adsorbents.

head.ce:title: Luminescent carbon dots-rooted polysaccharide crosslinked hydrogel adsorbent for sensitive determination and efficient removal of Cu2+

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: J.L. and L.W. contribute equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, a novel luminescent carbon dot-rooted polysaccharide hydrogel (CDs@CCP hydrogel) was prepared by crosslinking cellulose, chitosan (CS), and polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) for simultaneous fluorescent sensing and adsorption of Cu2+. The crosslinking of these low-cost, polysaccharide polymers greatly enhance the mechanical strength of the composite hydrogel while making the polysaccharide-based adsorbent easy to reuse. This composite hydrogel exhibited an excellent adsorption capacity (124.7 mg∙g−1) for residual Cu2+ in water, as well as a sensitive and selective fluorescence response towards Cu2+ with a good linear relationship (R2 > 0.97) and a low detection limit (LOD) of 0.02 μM. The adsorption isotherms, adsorption kinetics, and thermodynamics studies were also conducted to investigate the adsorption mechanism. This composite hydrogel offers an efficient tool for simultaneous monitoring and treatment of Cu2+ from wastewater.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The continuous global industrialization process has led to over-consumption of heavy metal-containing products, e.g., coal and oil, and excessive discharge of heavy metal elements such as Cu, Hg, Zn, Ni, Co, Cd, and Pb. Indisputably, these elements have been posing serious threats to ecological environment and human health due to their bioaccumulation effects and high toxicity (Mao, Yan, Shen, Zhang, & Zhang, 2021; Sarma, Sen Gupta, & Bhattacharyya, 2019). Cu, an abundant element in industrial raw materials and mines, widely exists in groundwater, surface water, and soils due to the emission of effluents (Vignardi et al., 2023; Wu et al., 2023). Although Cu is an essential trace element in human body, excessive accumulation of Cu elements in human body via food chains or intake of drinking water will cause serious health problems, such as gastrointestinal dysfunction, damage to the liver, kidney, and central nervous system (Gräf et al., 2023; Ibrahim, Abdulkarem, Naddeo, Banat, & Hasan, 2019). The World Health Organization (WHO) and Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) have recommended safe limits of Cu2+ in drinking water at 2 mg∙L−1 and 1.3 mg∙L−1, respectively (Zhang et al., 2023). Present strategies remediating metal ions-containing wastewater are diverse, exemplified by chemical precipitation, adsorption, electrolysis, ion exchange, membrane separation, and so on (Carolin, Kamalesh, Kumar, & Rangasamy, 2023; Kaur, Sengupta, & Mukhopadhyay, 2022), Among them, adsorption is deemed to be promising for recognizing and removing residual heavy metals from polluted water through developing cost-effective and versatile adsorbents which have displayed excellent removal ability even at low concentrations and the possibility of transforming residual metal ions into metal organic complex with high porosity and functional diversity (Praneeth, Zameer, Zhang, Dubey, & Sarmah, 2022; Wijeyawardana et al., 2022). Nowadays, various advanced materials have been employed as an adsorbent for remediating wastewater, such as covalent organic frameworks (COFs) (Zhu et al., 2023), carbon nanotubes (CNTs) (Ganzoury, Chidiac, Kurtz, & de Lannoy, 2020), and zeolites (Finish et al., 2023). However, these adsorbents have some shortcomings such as slow adsorption kinetics, high operating cost, non-biodegradability, and poor workability (Shahbazi, Jäger, Ahmadi, & Lacroix, 2020; Trikkaliotis, Christoforidis, Mitropoulos, & Kyzas, 2020). Therefore, novel materials with large surface area, high porosity, structural stability, biocompatibility, environmental friendliness, and renewability are still required for wastewater treatment (Song et al., 2023).

Hydrogels, a kind of polymeric materials with hydrophilic properties, have become an ideal substrate for constructing such effective and low-cost adsorbents due to their rigid three-dimensional crosslinking networks, high porosity, and ease of separation from sample matrix (Chen et al., 2022). Given the presence of hydrophilic groups such as hydroxy and carboxyl groups, hydrogels tend to combine with a large amount of water, which facilitates the dispersion and capture of heavy metal ions (Song et al., 2023). Cellulose-polyvinyl alcohol hydrogels show phenomenal adsorption capability for heavy metal ions due to the high degree of swelling and a large number of free hydroxyl groups (Tyagi & Thakur, 2023). However, aqueous matrix inevitably weakens the intermolecular interactions and leads to poor mechanical stability, limiting their popularization. Chitosan (CS), deacetylated derivative of natural chitin, is constituted of glucosamine and N-acetyl glucosamine linked in a β-(1–4) structural configurations, and is highly acclaimed due to its biodegradability, biocompatibility, and less chemical toxicity (Biswas & Biswas, 2023). The abundant amino groups in CS can share their lone pair electrons with protons to form cationic −NH3 + groups which can reduce the swelling ratio of hydrogels made by CS (Song et al., 2023). Notably, given the one-dimensional chain structures with plentiful hydroxyl and amino groups of these polysaccharides, exactly physical crosslinking via hydrogen bonding and/or chemical polymerization by adding inorganic or organic crosslinkers can construct a stable molecular skeleton into the hydrogels and provide sufficient active sites to anchor heavy metal ions or other functional materials, such as miniature luminescent carbon dot sensors.

Several functional materials such as 2,2,6,6-tetramehylpiperidine-1-oxyl radical (TEMPO)-oxidized cellulose nanofibers (TOCN) (Luo et al., 2021), carbon dots (Chen et al., 2022), and metal-organic framework (MOFs) (Li et al., 2020) have been extensively incorporated onto the skeletal network of hydrogels to improve functional diversity and enhance the adsorption performance. Carbon nanodots (CDs), novel zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials, have emerged as nontoxic and biocompatibility sensors for the sensing of heavy metal ions owing to their stimuli-responsive luminescent property and high chemical stability (Miao et al., 2018). However, in comparison with other zero-dimensional nanomaterials, the aggregation of CDs could lead to aggregation-induced quenching phenomenon, resulting in the loss of sensing ability (Wang et al., 2023). Hence, embedding predesigned CDs into polysaccharides-based hydrogels allows simultaneous adsorption and detection of a specific heavy metal ion through host-guest interactions, while avoiding aggregation-induced quenching effects of CDs. In this context, we make the hypothesis that the combination of CDs and polysaccharides-based hydrogels could construct a novel composite hydrogel adsorbent for efficient adsorption and detection of heavy metal ions in wastewater.

For this reason, we develop a new type of composite hydrogel (named CDs@CCP) with high fluorescence emission by integrating polysaccharides hydrogel and blue-emitting CDs. The amino-containing chitosan and hydroxyl-containing cellulose were used to construct the rigid skeleton of the composite hydrogel, and CDs provided functional sensing sites. This composite hydrogel not only exhibited excellent sensing ability towards trace level of Cu2+ via the specific host-guest interactions between surface functional groups of CDs and targeted Cu2+, but also a high adsorption capacity (124.7 mg∙g−1) for residual Cu2+ in water. A good linearity (0.05–5 μM, Cu2+ aqueous solution) with R2 > 0.97 and a comparable detection of limit (LOD) of 0.02 μM were obtained. We presumed that the chelating interaction between active groups and Cu2+ contributes to the high adsorption capacity. Hence, we investigated the adsorption mechanism by analyzing adsorption isotherms, adsorption kinetics, thermodynamics parameters, and infrared spectrum. It is expected that the nontoxic, eco-friendly biocompatible composite hydrogel can offer an efficient tool for the simultaneous monitoring and treatment of Cu2+ from wastewater.

Urea, full-hydrolyzed polyvinyl alcohol (PVA, M
w ≈ 30,000 g∙mol−1), and chitosan (M
w ≈ 300,000 g∙mol−1, deacetylation >85%) were obtained from Macklin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai city, China). Acetic acid, anhydrous citric acid, microcrystalline cellulose (90 μm of average particle size), epichlorohydrin (ECH), potassium chloride (KCl), sodium chloride (NaCl), barium chloride dihydrate (BaCl2∙2H2O), magnesium chloride hexahydrate (MgCl2·6H2O), zinc nitrate hexahydrate (Zn(NO3)2·6H2O), cadmium nitrate tetrahydrate (Cd(NO3)2·4H2O), copper chloride dihydrate (CuCl2·2H2O) and sodium hydroxide (NaOH) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All reagents were used without further purification.

The morphology of the composite hydrogel was analyzed by scanning electron microscopy (Hitachi Regulus8100, Japan). The functional groups of the hydrogels were investigated using Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR, Thermo Scientific Nicolet iS10, America). The crystallinity of the samples was measured using a powder X-ray diffraction spectrometer (PXRD, Bruker D2 PHASER, Germany). The fluorescence spectrum was measured using a fluorescence spectrophotometer (Shimadzu RF 6000, Japan). The concentration of metal ions in the solution was determined by Atomic Absorption Spectrophotometry (AA-240, Varian Corporation, USA).

The blue emissive CDs were synthesized using citric acids and urea as precursors according to a previous report (Miao et al., 2018). That is, 3 mmol citric acid and 9 mmol urea were dissolved in 15 mL deionized water and sonicated for 0.5 h. Then, the mixture was transferred into a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave, heated to 160 °C, and kept for 8 h. After cooling to room temperature, the mixture solution was centrifuged at 12000 rpm for 10 min to remove the solids. Finally, the supernatant was collected and dialyzed with a dialysis bag (500 Da) against deionized water for 12 h.

The CDs/cellulose/CS/PVA (CDs@CCP) composite hydrogels were synthesized by an in-situ chemical crosslinking method according to a previous study with slight modification (Chen, Huang, Luo, Zong, & Lou, 2021). The synthetic process is described in Fig. 1 . Firstly, a suspension of cellulose with a concentration of 4.0% (w/w) was prepared by dispersing cellulose nanowhiskers (CNW) (4 g) in a 6% (w/w) NaOH/12% (w/w) urea aqueous solution (96 mL). The obtained white suspension was vigorously stirred for 10 min and stored in a refrigerator for 12 h. Then, the frozen solid was thawed to room temperature and extensively stirred to obtain a transparent cellulose solution.

Secondly, 1 g of chitosan (CS) was dissolved in 50 mL of 2% (w/w) acetic acid aqueous solution under stirring. Separately, a 2% (w/w) polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) solution was prepared by dissolving 1 g of PVA in 49 mL of deionized water under stirring at 80 °C for 24 h. The CS and PVA solutions were mixed at a ratio of 1:1 by weight to obtain a mixed CS/PVA solution.

Thirdly, cellulose solution (60 mL) and CS/PVA solution (20 mL) were blended by a homogenizer to form a homogeneous cellulose/CS/PVA suspension. Then, 0.5 mL of epichlorohydrin (ECH, crosslinking agent) and 20 μL of the prepared CDs solution were added dropwise to the mixed suspension within 30 min. Finally, the suspension was poured into a glass plate and de-bubbled for 48 h and then dried at 60 °C in an oven for 24 h to obtain a composite hydrogel (denoted as CDs@CCP hydrogels). As a comparison, cellulose/CS/PVA (CCP) hydrogels were separately prepared in the same way as described above except that no CDs were added.

The sorption kinetics and isotherms of CDs@CCP hydrogels were examined by batch experiments. First, CuCl2·2H2O was used to prepare a Cu2+ solution with concentrations ranging from 25 to 1000 mg∙L−1. The uniformly divided CDs@CCP hydrogels (0.5 g) were added to a 250 mL flask containing the Cu2+ solution. Then, the sorption experiments were conducted in an incubator shaker at 25 °C with a speed of 150 rpm. The concentrations of residual ions were determined by an atomic absorption spectrophotometry (AA-240, Varian Corporation, USA). Three repetitive experiments were performed for verifying the experimental reproducibility. The adsorption capacities of hydrogels were calculated by Eq. (1):
(1)
q t
=
C 0
−
C 1
/ V
m
where
q t
(mg∙g−1) is the adsorption capacities of hydrogels,
C 0
(mg∙L−1) and
C 1
(mg∙L−1) are the concentrations of heavy metal ions before and after adsorption, respectively. m (g) and V (mL) are the mass of hydrogels and volume of the ion solutions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication and characterization of CDs@CCP hydrogels
ce:section.ce:para: The schematic for fabricating the fluorescent CDs@CCP hydrogels is shown in Fig. 1. Firstly, amino-rich chitosan and hydroxyl-rich cellulose were used to construct the skeletons of the porous adsorbents. Epichlorohydrin (ECH) was used as the crosslinking reagent. Given the full crosslinking reactions between precursors and crosslinking reagent, a porous and rigid network structure was obtained (Fig. 1). In addition, abundant hydrogen bond interactions (i.e., N∙∙∙∙∙∙H and O∙∙∙∙∙∙H) also strengthen the interlinked structures. Then, carbon dots were post-modified into the inner surfaces of the network structure by hydrogen bonds and/or chemical bridging via ECH, which enabled the fluorescent detection of heavy metal ions. Initially, the morphologies of the fluorescent CDs@CCP hydrogels are clearly shown in an SEM image (Fig. 2 ). It can be seen that the composite hydrogel has a three-dimensional network mesoporous structure with different porous sizes and volumes, which facilitates the diffusion and transport of heavy metal ions into its mass transfer channels and improves the adsorption capacity towards targeted ions.

The molecular interactions between functional groups of CDs, CCP, and CDs@CCP hydrogels were investigated by FT-IR spectroscopy, and the results are shown in Figs. 3a and b. In general, hydrogen bonding to a − OH or − NH2 group results in a decrease in its stretching frequency accompanied by a broadening of this band, as well as an intensification of the X − H (X means N or O) bands and weakening intensity of electron-donor groups (Larkin, 2011). Here, both −OH and − NH groups are found to show highly characteristic changes with hydrogen bonding in the FT-IR spectra of CDs@CCP hydrogels. A band at 3340 cm−1 assigned to the free −OH stretching mode and a band at 3186 cm−1 due to the free −NH2 stretching mode were observed in the FT-IR spectrum of CDs. However, in the FT-IR spectra of CCP and CDs@CCP hydrogels, the peak in the range of 3150–3400 cm−1 broadened out slightly due to the presence of abundant hydrogen bonds between −OH/−NH2 and electron-donating N or O elements in the CCP and CDs@CCP hydrogels. Additionally, due to the hydrogen bond interactions, two bands associated with the stretching mode of −NH2 were observed at lower frequencies (i.e., 2932 and 2877 cm−1) than the spectrum of CDs. As shown in Fig. 3b, a band at 1709 cm−1 due to the CO stretching mode and a band at 1592 cm−1 due to the NH bending and CN stretching modes were observed in both the FT-IR spectra of CDs and CDs@CCP hydrogels. Specifically, given the hydrogen bonding between CO groups in CDs and proton-donating −OH/−NH2 groups, the absorbance ratio of CO versus N-H/C-N (i.e., RatioC=O/N-H) in CDs@CCP hydrogels decreased from 0.39 to 0.28 after anchoring on the pore surface of the CCP hydrogels. All these changes in FT-IR spectra demonstrate that hydrogen bonding plays an important role in fabricating the mesoporous structure of CDs@CCP hydrogels.

The fabrication process of CDs@CCP hydrogels was also characterized by powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD) technology. As shown in Fig. 3c, chitosan exhibits two broad peaks at 10.7° and 19.8° which correspond to the semi-crystalline structure of chitosan (Guan et al., 2023). Two sharp peaks at 15.5° and 22.4° in the PXRD pattern of microcrystalline cellulose are attributed to the (101) and (002) planes, respectively, indicating a typical cellulose crystallinity of form I (Wan et al., 2014). Polyvinyl alcohol exhibits two main diffraction peaks at 19.5° and 40.3° which are attributed to its semi-crystalline region (Mahato, Sabbarwal, Misra, & Kumar, 2020). However, in the PXRD pattern of CDs@CCP hydrogels, the characteristic crystal planes of cellulose disappeared, which is attributed to the reconstruction of the intermolecular hydrogen bonding and crystalline structures in CDs@CCP hydrogels during the fabrication process. In addition, the new diffraction signal at 29.5° in the PXRD pattern of CDs@CCP hydrogel originates from the emerged semi-crystalline regions after the crosslinking reactions, indicating a successful construction of the mesoporous CDs@CCP hydrogel from its precursors.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties of CDs@CCP hydrogels and fluorescent detection of cu 2+
ce:section.ce:para: The CDs@CCP hydrogels were investigated for the capacity of detection of heavy metal ions. Fig. 3d shows the photoluminescent (PL) emission and excitation spectra of the composite hydrogel. The emission peak of the CDs@CCP composite hydrogel was observed at 370 nm under the maximum excitation wavelength of 290 nm. The UV–Vis spectrum (Fig. 3e) of neat CDs solution displays a main absorbance band at 275 nm, which is caused by the n-π* transition of the conjugated N- or O-containing functional groups on the surface of CDs (Chen et al., 2022). The maximum excitation wavelength at 290 nm is close to the absorbance band at 275 nm, indicating that the emission originated from the n-π* transition of conjugated C=O and CN groups in CDs. In addition, as shown in Fig. 3f, we observed a slight widening on the emission peak width after CDs encapsulated into the CCP hydrogels. This is due to the interactions between CDs and CCP composite hydrogel (i.e., electrostatic interaction and chemical crosslinking) that change the surface states of CDs and consequently increase the emission peak width of CDs@CCP hydrogel (Chen et al., 2022). These results also indicate that the photoluminescent property of CDs originates from the energy level recombination of the surface functional groups, rather than the energy level transition of the carbonized cores (Wang et al., 2016). The surface functional groups-induced luminescent property enables CDs@CCP hydrogel to act as a fluorescent sensor by disturbing the interactions between target analytes and functional groups of CDs in the hydrogel.

To evaluate the sensing ability of the composite hydrogel, CDs@CCP hydrogels were uniformly segmented into small cuboids (ca. 0.5 g per cuboid hydrogel) for further tests. Metal ion solution (50 mL) with different concentrations and an as-prepared cuboid hydrogel were placed into a flask and incubated at 25 °C for 1 h. Then, the fluorescence intensity at 370 nm of the hydrogels was recorded to evaluate the sensing ability of the hydrogels. Firstly, the selectivity of the CDs@CCP hydrogels to different metal ions (Ba2+、Cd2+、Cu2+、K+、Mg2+、Mn2+、Na+、Pb2+、Zn2+, 0.5 mM) was investigated through evaluating the fluorescent quenching efficiency ((I0 I)/I0). Among the tested ions, as shown in Fig. 4a, Cu2+ ion displayed the highest quenching efficiency (around 75%), while other metal ions showed quenching or enhancement effects lower than 20%, indicating that the CDs@CCP hydrogel could be employed as a sensitive Cu2+ ion sensor.

The fluorescence response of the CDs@CCP hydrogel to different concentrations of Cu2+ is shown in Fig. 4b. A good linear relationship (R2 = 0.9726) between the fluorescence quenching efficiency, i.e., (I0 I)/I0, and the concentrations of Cu2+ was obtained within the concentration range of 0.05–5 μM. The limit of detection (LOD) of the CDs@CCP hydrogel for Cu2+ was estimated to be 0.02 μM using the 3σ approach (σ = S/K, S represents the standard deviation of the blank samples, K is the slope of the working curve).

The adsorption performance of the CDs@CCP hydrogel on Cu2+ was further investigated. Initially, to investigate the effect of pH on the adsorption capacity of Cu2+, the pH values of Cu2+ aqueous solutions were set in the range of 2–7. Fig. S1 shows the effect of pH on the adsorption amount of Cu2+ by CDs@CCP hydrogels within the pH range of 2–7. The adsorption amount of the composite hydrogel is the smallest at pH = 2. As the pH value increases, the adsorption amount also increases gradually and peaks at pH = 5. At a low pH, the amino and hydroxyl groups in CDs@CCP hydrogels tend to be protonated, which provide electrostatic repulsion towards cationic Cu2+ and further result in a low adsorption amount. When pH = 6 and 7, the adsorption amount decreased slightly, which is attributed to the fact that the insoluble Cu(OH)2 tends to precipitate (Trikkaliotis et al., 2020; Zhong, Yue, Li, Gao, & Xu, 2014). Therefore, pH = 5 was selected for further adsorptive tests.

To understand the adsorption mechanism, the pseudo-first and pseudo-second-order adsorption kinetics models were constructed at three different concentrations (50, 200, and 300 mg∙L−1) according to Eqs. (2 and 3) (Fan et al., 2021; Zhong et al., 2014).
(2)
ln
q e
−
q t
= ln
q e
−
k 1
(3)
t
q t
=
1
q e 2
k 2
+
t
q e
where
q e
(mg∙g−1) is the equilibrium adsorption capacity;
q t
(mg∙g−1) is the adsorption capacity at time t ;
k 1
(min−1) and
k 2
(g∙mg−1∙min−1) represent the rate constants of pseudo-first-order and pseudo-second-order models, respectively.

The fitted curves are shown in Figs. 4c and d, and the fitted parameters are shown in Table S1. In comparison with the pseudo-first-order model, the pseudo-second-order kinetic model displays a higher correlation coefficient (R2 ≥ 0.999) for the adsorption of Cu2+ by the CDs@CCP adsorbents. Notably, the adsorption capacity (q
e cal , 5.09, 17.96, and 24.15 mg∙g−1) theoretically calculated by the pseudo-second-order model was comparable to the experimentally collected equilibrium adsorption capacity after 1 h of adsorption (q
e, exp , 5.12, 18.16 and 24.95 mg∙g−1), indicating a strong chemical interaction between Cu2+ and CDs@CCP adsorbents (Fan et al., 2021). In fact, the chelation of metal ions via -NH2, -NH-, and -OH groups in CDs@CCP hydrogels plays a significant role in the chemical interaction between adsorbents and targeted ions.

The adsorption isotherms were used to study the surface properties of adsorbents during the adsorption process and their adsorption mechanism. Herein, isothermal batch adsorption experiments were conducted under optimal conditions (pH = 5, Madsorbents = 0.5 g) with different Cu2+ concentrations (25–1000 mg∙L−1) at three temperatures (25, 35, and 45 °C). The adsorption isotherms were fitted according to Eqs. (4 and 5) (Fan et al., 2021).

Langmuir:
(4)
q e
=
q m
K L
C e
1 +
K L
C e

Freundlich:
(5)
q e
=
K F
C e
1 n
where
q e
(mg∙g−1) represents the adsorption capacity at equilibrium state;
q m
(mg∙g−1) represents the maximum adsorption capacity of the adsorbent; (mg∙g−1);
C e
(mg∙L−1) represents the concentration of the solution at adsorption equilibrium.
K L
and
K F
are the constants of Langmuir and Freundlich models, respectively; 1/n represents the surface heterogeneity of the adsorbent.

The adsorption isotherms and fitting parameters of CDs@CCP hydrogel adsorbents for the adsorption of Cu2+ are shown in Figs. 4e and f, as well as Table S2, respectively. Freundlich isotherm model (R2 ranging from 0.996 to 0.997) fitted better with the experimental data than Langmuir isotherm model (R2 ranging from 0.986 to 0.996), indicating that the adsorption process of Cu2+ is a composite multiplayer adsorption process (Chen et al., 2021; Khan, Badshah, & Airoldi, 2011). In addition, when the value of 1/n is in the range of 0.1–1, a favorable adsorption process tends to occur (Bulut, Gözübenli, & Aydın, 2007). As shown in Table S2, the 1/n values of the CDs@CCP hydrogels for Cu2+ adsorption were in the range of 0.491–0.586, indicating a favorable and heterogeneous adsorption. Additionally, the maximum adsorption capacity of CDs@CCP hydrogels for Cu2+ is 85.3 mg∙g−1, which is comparable with other cellulose and chitosan-based adsorbents (Table 1 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Thermodynamics studies of the adsorption process
ce:section.ce:para:
Thermodynamic studies were conducted under three different temperatures (298 K, 308 K, and 318 K). Gibbs function change ( ∆ G ), entropy change ( ∆ S ) and enthalpy change ( ∆ H ) were calculated by Van't Hoff Eqs. (6 and 7).
(6)
∆ G = − RT ln
K e
= ∆ H − T ∆ S
(7)
ln
q e
C e
= −
∆ H
RT
+
∆ S
R
where ∆ G (kJ∙mol−1) is the Gibbs function change; ∆ S (J∙K−1∙mol−1) is the change in entropy; ∆ H (kJ∙mol−1) is the enthalpy change; T (K) represents the adsorption temperature; R represents the universal gas constant (8.314 J∙K−1∙mol−1). The values of ∆ H and ∆ S were calculated from the slope and intercept of the plot ln
q e
C e
versus 1 / T , respectively (Trikkaliotis et al., 2020). The estimated thermodynamic parameters are listed in Table S3. The Cu2+ adsorption process using CDs@CCP hydrogels as adsorbents exhibits negative ∆ G values, indicating a spontaneous adsorption process (Chen et al., 2021; Chen et al., 2022). The negative values of ∆ H (−10.34 kJ∙mol−1) indicate that the adsorption of Cu2+ is an exothermic process (Yan et al., 2011).

The recycling tests were conducted under the optimal conditions (200 mg∙L−1 of Cu2+ solution, pH = 5, 25 °C). A 0.05 mol∙L−1 Na2EDTA aqueous solution was used to desorb metal ions from the hydrogel. The desorption process just takes 2 h to extensively remove residue metal ions. As shown in Fig. S2, the adsorption capacity of CDs@CCP hydrogel after five cycles remained 90% of its initial adsorption capacity, indicating a satisfactory reusability.

The adsorption process is complicated due to the simultaneous presence of physical and chemical interactions between adsorbents and targeted ions. On the one hand, due to the large amounts of functional groups, i.e., primary amine (−NH2), secondary amine (−C − NH − C−), hydroxyl (−OH) and ether (−C − O − C−) groups, CDs@CCP hydrogels can theoretically adsorb heavy metal ions via chelation (Li, Liang, Mao, & Li, 2022; Liu, Zhang, Liu, & Zhang, 2023). The nitrogen and oxygen atoms can tightly bind Cu2+ by sharing their lone electron pairs, leading to the formation of a stable coordination complex. Furthermore, the efficient chemisorption between Cu2+ and CDs@CCP hydrogels is significantly facilitated by the presence of numerous chelating sites, i.e., nitrogen and oxygen atoms of functional groups. When Cu2+ is combined with CDs@CCP hydrogels, Cu2+ is easily precipitated by forming a Cu-CDs@CCP complex ([Cu(NH2)(OH)nX], where X is H3O+). In order to explore the interactions between CDs@CCP hydrogels and Cu2+, the FT-IR spectra of CDs@CCP hydrogel before and after adsorption of Cu2+ were recorded. As shown in Fig. 5b, the band at 1592 cm−1 (NH bending and CN stretching modes) disappeared after adsorbing Cu2+, confirming the presence of a chemisorption process. On the other hand, the adsorption process is also governed by the hydrogen bonding interactions and electrostatic interactions between CDs@CCP hydrogels and Cu2+ (Fig. 5a). At a low pH (pH = 5), the -NH2 groups are protonated which lead to electrostatic interactions between CDs@CCP hydrogels and corresponding copper anions. This is mainly the result of metal chelation by chloride ions and anionic ligands, etc. (Liu et al., 2023; Pavithra et al., 2021; Vieira, Sanches Neto, Carvalho-Silva, & Signini, 2019).

In conclusion, a novel polysaccharides-based CDs@CCP composite hydrogel with bright blue fluorescence was successfully prepared by embedding fluorescent carbon dots. The adsorption kinetics, thermodynamics, and isothermal adsorption model experiments show that the adsorption of Cu2+ by CDs@CCP composite hydrogel followed Freundlich and pseudo-second-order models, indicating that the adsorption of Cu2+ is an exothermic process, whereas chemical adsorption can be attributed to electron transfer or electron pair sharing. Chelation, hydrogen bonding, and electrostatic interaction offered efficient adsorption between CDs@CCP hydrogel and Cu2+. Most importantly, the fluorescence detection experiments exhibited a good linearity with R2 > 0.97 in the range of 0.05–5 μM. The detection limit of CDs@CCP composite hydrogel for Cu2+ was estimated to be 0.02 μM using 3σ method. In addition, the CDs@CCP can be easily reused through an easy recycling process, which is a significant characteristic of a qualified adsorbent. Compared with other hydrogels, the composite hydrogel is endowed with multi-functions, good reusability, high stability, and efficient adsorption ability. We successfully proved the hypothesis that the CDs@CCP hydrogel-based adsorbent presented in this work offers a great potential for the detection and adsorption of Cu2+ from wastewater as the stable structure, stimuli-responsive fluorescence, and sufficient binding sites. This also represents a very promising step for further development of this material to achieve simultaneous monitoring and treatment for other relevant heavy metal ions.

Jingkun Li: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Liying Wang: Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Guoyong Jiang: Formal analysis, Data curation. Yuqi Wan: Formal analysis, Data curation. Jiahua Wang: Writing – review & editing. Yan Li: Writing – review & editing. Fuwei Pi: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Methodology, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare no competing interests.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the financial support from the Postgraduate Research & Practice Innovation Program of Jiangsu Province (No. KYCX22_2400).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2024.138977.
